perturbation.original_text: b) Governing Law. This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, notwithstanding the actual state or country of residence or incorporation of Excite or Client.
perturbation.changed_text: b) Governing Law. This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by Excite, notwithstanding the actual state or country of residence or incorporation of Excite or Client.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law provision to allow Excite to choose the jurisdiction removes the consumer's right to have the contract governed by a predictable and relevant legal framework. This could potentially violate consumer protection laws if Excite chooses a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protections than New York or the consumer's state of residence.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 399-c addresses choice of law clauses in consumer contracts. While it doesn't explicitly prohibit selecting a different state's law, it implies that the selected law should not deprive the consumer of protections afforded by New York law. This clause, by allowing Excite to choose any jurisdiction, could be used to circumvent NY law, potentially depriving New York consumers of rights they would otherwise have. It also creates ambiguity and potential hardship for consumers who may have to litigate in a distant and unfamiliar jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle that consumers should not be deprived of the protections afforded by New York law. By allowing Excite to choose any jurisdiction, the modified contract introduces the risk that a jurisdiction with weaker consumer protections could be selected, thereby circumventing New York law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly prohibit selecting a different state's law, the Law Explanation implies the selected law should not deprive the consumer of protections afforded by New York law. The changed text contradicts this principle.

perturbation.original_text:
13. INDEMNITY
a) Client will indemnify, defend and hold harmless Excite, its affiliates, officers, directors, employees, consultants and agents from any and all third party claims, liability, damages and/or costs (including, but not limited to, attorneys fees) arising from:
i) The breach of any representation or covenant in this Agreement; or
ii) Any claim that Client's advertising banners infringe or violate any third party's copyright, patent, trade secret, trademark, right of publicity or right of privacy or contain any defamatory content; or
iii) Any claim arising from content displayed on the Client Site. Excite will promptly notify Client of any and all such claims and will reasonably cooperate with Client with the defense and/or settlement thereof; provided that, if any settlement requires an affirmative obligation of, results in any ongoing liability to or prejudices or detrimentally impacts Excite in any way and such obligation, liability, prejudice or impact can reasonably be expected to be material, then such settlement shall require Excite's written consent (not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed) and Excite may have its own counsel in attendance at all proceedings and substantive negotiations relating to such claim.
perturbation.changed_text:
13. INDEMNITY
a) Client will indemnify, defend and hold harmless Excite, its affiliates, officers, directors, employees, consultants and agents from any and all third party claims, liability, damages and/or costs (including, but not limited to, attorneys fees) arising from:
i) The breach of any representation or covenant in this Agreement; or
ii) Any claim that Client's advertising banners infringe or violate any third party's copyright, patent, trade secret, trademark, right of publicity or right of privacy or contain any defamatory content; or
iii) Any claim arising from content displayed on the Client Site. Excite will have no obligation to notify Client of any such claims, and Client will be solely responsible for the defense and/or settlement thereof, regardless of impact on Excite.
perturbation.explanation: By removing Excite's obligation to notify Client of third-party claims and removing the requirement for Client's written consent for settlements that materially impact Excite, the contract shifts disproportionate risk onto the Client, which may violate fairness and good faith requirements implied in New York contract law, especially if the Client is a smaller entity. This clause now places an unreasonable burden on Client, and could potentially obligate the client to settle disputes that are harmful to Excite.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause that is found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The modified indemnity clause, by placing an unreasonable burden and risk on the client, could be deemed unconscionable, particularly considering the power imbalance between a large company like Excite and potentially smaller businesses that might advertise on its platform.

Unconscionable Contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts NY UCC § 2-302 regarding unconscionable contract clauses. By removing Excite's obligation to notify the Client of claims and by placing sole responsibility for defense and settlement on the Client, the revised clause places an unreasonable risk and burden on the Client. This is especially relevant considering the potential power imbalance between Excite and its advertising clients. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, 'If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract...'. This indicates a contradiction because the modified clause could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, as it substantially shifts the risk to the client and removes a reasonable notification requirement from Excite.

perturbation.original_text:
14. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY
EXCEPT UNDER SECTIONS 13(a) AND 13(b), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES, WHETHER BASED ON BREACH OF CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE) OR OTHERWISE, WHETHER OR NOT THAT PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE LIABILITY OF EITHER PARTY FOR DAMAGES OR ALLEGED DAMAGES HEREUNDER, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR ANY OTHER LEGAL THEORY, IS LIMITED TO, AND WILL NOT EXCEED, THE AMOUNTS TO BE PAID BY CLIENT TO EXCITE HEREUNDER.
perturbation.changed_text:
14. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY
IN NO EVENT WILL EXCITE BE LIABLE TO THE CLIENT FOR ANY DAMAGES, WHETHER BASED ON BREACH OF CONTRACT, TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE) OR OTHERWISE, WHETHER OR NOT EXCITE HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE LIABILITY OF EXCITE FOR DAMAGES OR ALLEGED DAMAGES HEREUNDER, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR ANY OTHER LEGAL THEORY, IS LIMITED TO, AND WILL NOT EXCEED, $100.
perturbation.explanation: By unilaterally limiting Excite's liability to a nominal amount ($100) while removing any reciprocal limitation for the Client, the contract becomes grossly unfair and potentially unconscionable. This significant disparity in liability violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing inherent in New York contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-719 governs contractual modification or limitation of remedy. While it allows for limitations of liability, subsection (3) states that consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limiting Excite's liability to $100, regardless of the damages caused, while placing no corresponding limit on the client's potential liability under the indemnity clause, is likely to be deemed unconscionable, especially if the actual damages suffered by the client are substantially greater than $100. This clause makes the agreement one-sided in favor of Excite.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 7 Remedies previous SECTION 2-718 Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits up PART 7 Remedies next SECTION 2-720 Effect of "Cancellation" or "Rescission" on Claims for Antecedent Breach This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-719 Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 7 Section 2--719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not.
perturbation.justification: The changed contract text creates a legal contradiction by significantly limiting Excite's liability to a nominal $100 while not placing a similar limit on the Client's liability. This alteration is addressed in NY UCC § 2-719, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses contractual modification or limitation of remedy. While subsection (1)(a) allows for limitations, subsection (3) notes that consequential damages may be limited unless the limitation is unconscionable. The changed text makes the agreement one-sided, potentially deeming the liability limitation unconscionable if the client's damages substantially exceed $100. The modification undermines the principle of fair dealing and good faith, creating a clear contradiction by omitting a reciprocal limitation of liability, thus favoring one party over the other in a way that could be considered unconscionable under the UCC.
